name: Menstrual Health
meta_description: Learn about menstrual health: cycle phases, common irregularities, symptoms, and when to seek care for abnormal periods.
description: Menstrual health refers to the overall well-being of the menstrual cycle, including cycle regularity, flow, and the absence of severe symptoms that interfere with daily life.
overview: Menstrual health encompasses the regular functioning of the menstrual cycle, which is a key indicator of overall reproductive and hormonal health. A typical menstrual cycle lasts between 21 and 35 days, with bleeding lasting 2 to 7 days. Variations in cycle length, flow, and associated symptoms are common, but significant changes or severe symptoms may signal underlying conditions such as hormonal imbalances, polyps, fibroids, or thyroid disorders. Tracking the cycle and understanding what is normal for your body empowers early detection of issues and supports informed conversations with healthcare providers.
risk_factors: Eating disorders or low body weight
lifestyle_tips: Track your cycle using an app or calendar to identify patterns
Maintain a balanced diet rich in iron, calcium, and omega-3 fatty acids
Stay hydrated and limit caffeine and salt before your period
Engage in regular, moderate exercise to reduce cramps and mood symptoms
Practice stress-reduction techniques such as yoga or mindfulness
Use heat therapy (heating pad) for cramp relief
Prioritize sleep to support hormonal balance
when_to_seek_care: Consult a healthcare provider if your periods are consistently shorter than 21 days or longer than 35 days, bleeding lasts more than 7 days, you soak through a pad or tampon every hour for several hours, you experience severe pain that disrupts daily activities, or you miss periods for three months in a row (and are not pregnant). Seek immediate care for sudden heavy bleeding with dizziness or fainting.

name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
meta_description: Learn about PCOS: symptoms, hormonal causes, long-term risks, and lifestyle-based management strategies.
description: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a common hormonal disorder in women of reproductive age, characterized by irregular periods, excess androgens, and polycystic ovaries.
overview: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is one of the most common endocrine disorders affecting women of reproductive age, with an estimated prevalence of 6 to 12 percent worldwide. It is defined by a combination of irregular menstrual cycles, elevated androgen (male hormone) levels, and polycystic-appearing ovaries on ultrasound. PCOS is a leading cause of infertility and is closely linked to insulin resistance, which increases the risk of type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular disease. While there is no cure, early diagnosis and ongoing management through lifestyle changes, medication, and regular monitoring can effectively control symptoms and reduce long-term health risks.
common_symptoms: Weight gain or difficulty losing weight
Darkening of skin in folds (acanthosis nigricans)
risk_factors: Type 2 diabetes or gestational diabetes in family
Higher levels of androgens during puberty
lifestyle_tips: Focus on weight management; even a 5-10% weight loss can restore ovulation
Adopt a low-glycemic-index diet rich in whole grains, vegetables, and lean protein
Engage in at least 150 minutes of moderate exercise per week
Include strength training to improve insulin sensitivity
Limit processed foods and added sugars
Work with an endocrinologist or gynecologist on medication options
Monitor mental health, as anxiety and depression are more common in PCOS
when_to_seek_care: See a healthcare provider if you have irregular or absent periods, unexplained weight gain with acne or excess hair growth, or difficulty getting pregnant. Seek urgent care for sudden severe pelvic pain, especially with vomiting, which can indicate ovarian torsion. Regular check-ups are important to screen for diabetes, high blood pressure, and cholesterol if you have PCOS.

name: Endometriosis
meta_description: Learn about endometriosis: symptoms, causes, diagnosis, and treatment options for this chronic pelvic condition.
description: Endometriosis is a chronic condition in which tissue similar to the lining of the uterus grows outside the uterus, causing pain and potential fertility issues.
overview: Endometriosis is a chronic, often painful condition in which endometrial-like tissue grows outside the uterus, most commonly on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic lining. This tissue responds to hormonal changes, causing inflammation, scarring, and adhesions. The condition affects roughly 1 in 10 women of reproductive age and is a leading cause of infertility. Symptoms vary widely and do not always correlate with disease severity, which contributes to an average diagnostic delay of 7 to 10 years. While endometriosis has no cure, a combination of medical therapy, surgery, and lifestyle strategies can effectively manage pain and preserve fertility.
common_symptoms: Chronic pelvic or lower back pain
Painful bowel movements or urination during periods
Digestive issues such as bloating, diarrhea, or constipation
risk_factors: Heavy periods lasting more than 7 days
lifestyle_tips: Apply heat (heating pads, warm baths) to relieve pelvic pain
Follow an anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3s, fruits, and vegetables
Limit red meat, caffeine, and alcohol, which may worsen inflammation
Engage in gentle, regular exercise such as yoga, walking, or swimming
Practice stress management through meditation or cognitive behavioral therapy
Keep a symptom and pain diary to share with your care team
Consider pelvic floor physical therapy for muscle tension relief
when_to_seek_care: Seek evaluation for pelvic pain that disrupts daily life, painful periods that do not respond to over-the-counter pain relief, pain during intercourse, or difficulty becoming pregnant. Seek immediate care for sudden severe abdominal pain, especially with fever or vomiting, which can signal a ruptured cyst or ovarian torsion.

name: Menopause
meta_description: Learn about menopause: stages, common symptoms, long-term health impacts, and management strategies.
description: Menopause is the natural transition marking the end of menstrual cycles, typically occurring between ages 45 and 55, accompanied by hormonal and physical changes.
overview: Menopause is defined as the permanent cessation of menstruation, confirmed after 12 consecutive months without a period. It typically occurs between ages 45 and 55, with the average age around 51. The transition, called perimenopause, can last several years and is marked by fluctuating estrogen and progesterone levels. These hormonal changes produce a range of symptoms including hot flashes, night sweats, sleep disturbances, mood changes, and vaginal dryness. Menopause also increases the risk of osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease due to the protective effects of estrogen being lost. While menopause is a natural life stage, a range of lifestyle, non-hormonal, and hormonal therapies can ease symptoms and protect long-term health.
common_symptoms: Weight gain, particularly around the abdomen
risk_factors: Smoking, which can trigger earlier menopause
Certain cancer treatments such as chemotherapy or radiation
lifestyle_tips: Eat a diet rich in calcium and vitamin D to protect bone health
Engage in weight-bearing and resistance exercise to prevent osteoporosis
Dress in layers and keep the bedroom cool to manage hot flashes
Limit caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods, which can trigger hot flashes
Practice relaxation techniques such as deep breathing and yoga
Maintain a consistent sleep schedule and good sleep hygiene
Stay connected with a healthcare provider to discuss hormone or non-hormonal therapies
when_to_seek_care: Talk to a healthcare provider if symptoms such as hot flashes, sleep problems, or mood changes interfere with daily life, or if you experience bleeding after menopause, which should always be evaluated. Seek urgent care for heavy bleeding, severe pelvic pain, or signs of cardiovascular issues such as chest pain or shortness of breath.

name: Breast Health
meta_description: Learn about breast health: screening, common changes, risk factors, and early detection of breast conditions.
description: Breast health involves awareness of normal breast changes, routine screening, and early detection of conditions ranging from benign cysts to breast cancer.
overview: Breast health refers to the ongoing awareness, screening, and care of breast tissue throughout a woman's life. Breasts undergo normal changes influenced by hormones, age, and life events such as pregnancy and menopause. Understanding what is normal for your body allows earlier detection of unusual changes, which is critical because breast cancer is the most commonly diagnosed cancer in women worldwide. Early detection through self-awareness, clinical exams, and mammography significantly improves outcomes. Most breast changes are benign, including cysts and fibrocystic changes, but any new or persistent change should be evaluated promptly by a healthcare professional.
common_symptoms: A new lump or thickening in the breast or underarm
Change in breast size, shape, or contour
Nipple discharge, especially if bloody or clear
Scaliness or rash on the nipple
Swelling or warmth in part of the breast
risk_factors: Family history of breast or ovarian cancer
Inherited mutations such as BRCA1 or BRCA2
Early menstruation (before age 12) or late menopause (after age 55)
lifestyle_tips: Perform regular breast self-awareness to notice changes early
Follow recommended screening guidelines for mammograms and clinical exams
Maintain a healthy weight, especially after menopause
Limit alcohol to no more than one drink per day
Engage in regular physical activity
Eat a diet rich in vegetables, fruits, and whole grains
Discuss personal risk factors with your healthcare provider to plan screening
when_to_seek_care: See a healthcare provider promptly if you notice a new lump, a change in breast size or shape, nipple discharge (especially bloody or clear), skin changes such as dimpling or redness, or persistent pain in one area. Never delay evaluation of a change that persists through one full menstrual cycle. Seek urgent care for rapidly growing, painful swelling with fever, which may indicate an infection (mastitis) or abscess.
